name: Pink Slime Controversy
description: The "Pink Slime Controversy" refers to the public debate surrounding the use of Lean Finely Textured Beef (LFTB), often referred to as "pink slime," in ground beef production in the United States. Proponents within the meat industry argue that LFTB is a safe and cost-effective way to increase the yield of usable meat from beef trimmings, reducing waste and lowering prices for consumers. They maintain that it meets all USDA safety standards and is nutritionally equivalent to other ground beef components. The controversy erupted in 2012, fueled by media coverage and public concern about the perceived lack of transparency regarding its inclusion in ground beef sold in supermarkets and school lunch programs. Critics argued that LFTB is not properly labeled, is aesthetically unappealing, and is treated with ammonia gas to kill bacteria, raising concerns about potential health risks and ethical considerations. Mainstream perspectives, including the USDA and many food safety experts, emphasize that LFTB is a safe and acceptable ingredient when produced according to established regulations. They point to the use of ammonia as an effective antimicrobial agent and highlight the rigorous testing and inspection processes in place. However, the public perception, heavily influenced by the media's portrayal of LFTB as an undesirable byproduct, led to significant consumer backlash and a decline in its use. The controversy also sparked legal battles, including a defamation lawsuit against ABC News by Beef Products, Inc. (BPI), a major LFTB producer, which was eventually settled. The lasting impact of the "pink slime" controversy is increased consumer awareness and demand for transparency in food production, as well as a greater scrutiny of the ingredients and processes used in the meat industry.
subtitle: Debate over Lean Finely Textured Beef

claims.claim: Pink slime is unsafe for consumption.
claims.evidence: Concerns about the ammonia treatment and potential for contamination.
claims.criticism: The USDA and other food safety agencies maintain that the ammonia treatment is safe and effective in killing bacteria, and LFTB undergoes rigorous testing.

claims.claim: Pink slime is deceptively added to ground beef without proper labeling.
claims.evidence: Lack of clear labeling on ground beef products indicating the presence of LFTB.
claims.criticism: While specific labeling requirements may vary, the USDA has regulations regarding ingredient labeling. The industry argues that LFTB is considered part of the ground beef and doesn't require separate labeling.

claims.claim: Pink slime is a lower-quality meat product.
claims.evidence: It is made from beef trimmings and treated with ammonia, implying a lower standard than regular ground beef.
claims.criticism: Industry proponents argue that LFTB is nutritionally similar to other ground beef components and provides a cost-effective way to utilize beef trimmings, reducing waste.

keyFigures.name: Gerald Zirnstein
keyFigures.role: Former USDA scientist who used the term 'pink slime'

keyFigures.name: Beef Products, Inc. (BPI)
keyFigures.role: Major producer of Lean Finely Textured Beef (LFTB)

timeline.event: LFTB becomes more widely used in the beef industry.
The "pink slime" controversy erupts following media coverage.
Beef Products, Inc. settles defamation lawsuit against ABC News.

sources.author: Michael Moss
sources.work: Salt Sugar Fat: How the Food Giants Hooked Us
sources.title: Michael Moss

sources.author: USDA

sources.title: USDA
